text: The surgeon uses robotic instruments to open the pars flaccida, providing a clear view of the left gastric vessel and the underside of the stomach.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to dissect the mesentery near the left gastric vessel. A 45mm intelligent stapler is inserted and fired to create a gastric pouch, followed by the insertion of a bougie. Fat is dissected over the greater curvature, and subsequent stapler firings complete the formation of a small, vertical gastric pouch.

A surgical drain is placed around the small bowel at approximately 150 centimeters, followed by splitting the bowel to create a Roux limb. The surgeon passes a non-absorbable suture to form the posterior layer of the gastrojejunostomy.

The surgeon uses a continuous V-lock suture to finish the posterior layer, ensuring there is no tension between the small bowel and the gastric pouch. An enterotomy is made on the gastric pouch and the rollem, with a stapler introduced into the surgical field.

The surgeon closes the enterotomy using absorbable 2-0 V-lock sutures, starting at two ends and tying at the center. The enterotomy site is then closed with a fourth layer of non-absorbable V-lock suture to bury the staple line, minimizing the chance of leakage. The robotic sewing is precise and hemostatic.
The surgeon completes the fourth layer of suturing to finalize the gastro-jejunostomy, ensuring the connection between the gastric pouch and the jejunum is secure.

The surgeon measures the length of the Roux limb and creates an enterotomy at 150 centimeters using a hook. The biliary enteric limb is visible. An articulating stapler, 45 millimeters in length, is used to insert the two limbs easily without assistance.

The surgeon fires the stapler to establish a side-to-side connection between the gastric pouch and the roux limb. The enterotomy is closed using an absorbable 2-0 V-lock suture, ensuring good hemostasis with no visible bleeding. The robotic arm assists in aligning the tissues efficiently. An endoscope is passed to check the anastomosis, using air insufflation for visualization.
